## INTSGD: ADAPTIVE FLOATLESS COMPRESSION OF STOCHASTIC GRADIENTS

## **Konstantin Mishchenko**

CNRS, École Normale Supérieure, Inria konsta.mish@gmail.com

**Bokun Wang** KAUST _[∗]_

bokunw.wang@gmail.com

**Dmitry Kovalev Peter Richtárik** KAUST KAUST dakovalev1@gmail.com peter.richtarik@kaust.edu.sa

## ABSTRACT

We propose a family of adaptive integer compression operators for distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) that do not communicate a single float. This is achieved by multiplying floating-point vectors with a number known to every device and then rounding to integers. In contrast to the prior work on integer compression for SwitchML by Sapio et al. (2021), our IntSGD method is provably convergent and computationally cheaper as it estimates the scaling of vectors adaptively. Our theory shows that the iteration complexity of IntSGD matches that of SGD up to constant factors for both convex and non-convex, smooth and nonsmooth functions, with and without overparameterization. Moreover, our algorithm can also be tailored for the popular all-reduce primitive and shows promising empirical performance.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Many recent breakthroughs in machine learning were made possible due to the introduction of large, sophisticated and high capacity supervised models whose training requires days or even weeks of computation (Hinton et al., 2015; He et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2018). However, it would not be possible to train them without corresponding advances in parallel and distributed algorithms capable of taking advantage of modern hardware. Very large models are typically trained on vast collections of training data stored in a distributed fashion across a number of compute nodes that need to communicate throughout the training process. In this scenario, reliance on efficient communication protocols is of utmost importance.

**Communication in distributed systems.** The training process of large models relies on fast synchronization of gradients computed in a parallel fashion. Formally, to train a model, we want to solve the problem of parallel/distributed minimization of the average of _n_ functions:

where we will compute the gradients of stochastic realizations _fi_ ( _x_ ; _ξ_ ). The two dominating protocols for gradient synchronization are _all-reduce_ and _all-gather_ aggregation, which may use either Parameter Server or all-to-all communication under the hood. The core difference between them lies in that all-gather communicates all vectors, whereas all-reduce only outputs their average. As shown in previous works, current distributed deep learning algorithms predominantly use all-reduce as it scales much better than all-gather (Vogels et al., 2019; Agarwal et al., 2021).

A popular way to reduce the communication cost of both all-reduce and all-gather primitives is to use lossy compression of gradients (Ramesh et al., 2021). To study the benefit of lossy compression, large swaths of recent literature on distributed training attribute the cost of sending a single vector

from a worker to the server to the number of bits needed to represent it. Based on this abstraction, various elaborate vector compression techniques (see Table 1 in Beznosikov et al. 2020; Xu et al. 2020; Safaryan et al. 2020) and algorithms have been designed for higher and higher compression ratios. However, in real systems, the efficiency of sending a vector is not fully characterized by the number of bits alone, because:

- First, many compressors with high compression ratio (e.g., natural compression (Horváth et al., 2019), quantization (Alistarh et al., 2017), top- _k_ sparsification, sign (Bernstein et al., 2018)) are not compatible with the efficient all-reduce primitive and require all-gather implementation.

- Secondly, some compressors rely on expensive operations such as low-rank decomposition (Wang et al., 2018; Vogels et al., 2019) or bit-level operations (Horváth et al., 2019), whose computation overhead may outweigh the benefits of reduced communication load.

- Thirdly, algorithms with biased compressors such as Top- _k_ SGD, SignSGD, PowerSGD (Vogels et al., 2019), require the error-feedback (EF-SGD) mechanism (Stich et al., 2018; Karimireddy et al., 2019) to ensure the convergence. Alas, error feedback needs extra sequences that may not fit the low memory budget of GPUs. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, no convergence guarantee has been established for EF-SGD on the non-smooth objectives with multiple workers.

**SwitchML.** Another approach to combating long communication times is to improve the hardware itself. The recently proposed SwitchML is an alternative to the NVIDIA Collective Communications Library (NCCL) on real-world hardware (Sapio et al., 2021). The first key component of SwitchML is the in-network aggregation (INA) by a programmable switch. INA reduces the communication cost and latency because the execution can be paralleled and pipelined. To be specific, it splits the vector to aggregate into chunks and processes them individually by the switch pipeline. The advantages of INA over parameter server and all-reduce in terms of latency and communication cost have been theoretically and empirically justified by Sapio et al. (2021). The second key component of SwitchML is stochastic gradient descent with integer rounding and aggregation. Instead of reducing the data volume to exchange, the goal of integer rounding in SwitchML is to fit the limited computation capability of the modern programmable switch, which only supports integer additions or logic operations. To increase the rounding precision, the gradient _gi[k]_[on device] _[ i]_[ multiplies a positive] scaling factor _αk_ known to every worker and then rounded to an integer number _Int_ ( _αk ◦ gi[k]_[)][.][As] there is no additional scaling or decompression before aggregating the communicated vectors, their sums can be computed on the fly. Then, each worker can divide the aggregated gradient by _nαk_ to update the model.

However, Sapio et al. (2021) remark that the choice of the scaling factor _αk_ requires special care. In their presentation[1] , one of the authors notes: “A bad choice of scaling factor can reduce the performance.” To this end, they propose a heuristic-based profiling step that is executed before the gradient aggregation and keeps the rounded integers small to fit in 32 bits. We refer to their algorithm including the profiling step as Heuristic IntSGD. Unfortunately, no convergence guarantee for that algorithm has been established. This is where our theory comes to the rescue. By rigorously and exhaustively analyzing integer rounding based on scaling, we find adaptive rules for the scaling factor _αk_ that do not require the profiling employed by Sapio et al. (2021). As we will show in the remainder of the paper, our algorithm is perfectly suited for both in-network aggregation (INA) of SwitchML and for other efficient primitives such as all-reduce.

## 1.1 CONTRIBUTIONS

We summarize the key differences of our algorithm and prior work in Table 1, and we also list our main contributions below.

_•_ **Adaptive IntSGD.** We develop a family of computationally cheap adaptive scaling factors for provably convergent IntSGD. It is a better alternative to the Heuristic IntSGD in Sapio et al. (2021) that requires expensive operations and does not ensure convergence.

> (1) In theory, PowerSGD requires computing low-rank decompositions. In practice, an approximation is found by power iteration, which requires just a few matrix-vector multiplications but it is not analyzed theoretically and might be less stable.

> (2) PowerSGD requires tuning the rank of the low-rank decomposition. Vogels et al. (2019) reported that rank-1 PowerSGD consistently underperformed in their experiments, and, moreover, rank-2 was optimal for image classification while language modeling required rank-4. Ramesh et al. (2021) reported that a much larger rank was needed to avoid a gap in the training loss.

_•_ **Rates.** We obtain the first analysis of the integer rounding and aggregation for distributed machine learning. For all of the proposed variants, we prove convergence rates of IntSGD that match those of full-precision SGD up to constant factors. Our results are tight and apply to both convex and nonconvex problems. Our analysis does not require any extra assumption compared to those typically invoked for SGD. In contrast to other compression-based methods, IntSGD has the same rate as that of full-precision SGD even on non-smooth problems.

_•_ **IntDIANA.** We observe empirically that IntSGD struggles when the devices have heterogeneous (non-identical) data—an issue it shares with vanilla SGD—and propose an alternative method, IntDIANA, that can provably alleviate this issue. We also show that our tools are useful for extending the methodology beyond SGD methods, for example, to _variance reduced_ methods (Johnson & Zhang, 2013; Allen-Zhu & Hazan, 2016; Kovalev et al., 2020; Gower et al., 2020) with integer rounding. Please refer to Appendix A.2 for theoretical results and Appendix C.5 for the empirical verification.

## 3 ANALYSIS OF INTSGD[2]

To establish convergence of IntSGD, we introduce the following assumption on the scaling vector _αk_ = ( _αk,_ 1 _, . . . , αk,d_ ) _[⊤] ∈_ R _[d]_ ++[.]

While this assumption may look exotic, it captures precisely what we need to establish the convergence of IntSGD, and it holds for several practical choices of _αk_ , including the one shown in Section 4 and more choices in Appendix A.1.

**Challenges in IntSGD analysis.** Although the _Int_ operation is unbiased and has finite variance as shown in Lemma 1, we highlight that it is non-trivial to obtain the convergence analysis of IntSGD and the analysis is different from that of QSGD and similar methods. Indeed, QSGD, Rank- _k_ , and NatSGD all use unbiased operators _Q_ with variance satisfying E[ _∥Q_ ( _g_ ) _− g∥_[2] ] _≤ ω∥g∥_[2] for some _ω >_ 0. For them, the convergence theory is simply a plug-in of the analysis of Compressed SGD (Khirirat et al., 2018). However, the integer rounding operator _Int_ does not satisfy this property, and the variance of the integer compressor will not decrease to zero when _∥g∥_[2] _→_ 0. Moreover, to estimate _αk_ adaptively, we use the values of past iterates, which makes its value itself random, so the analysis of Compressed SGD cannot apply. As shown in our proofs, an extra trick is required: we reserve an additional term[�] _[k] t_ =0[E][[] _[∥][x][t]_[+1] _[ −][x][t][∥]_[2][]][ to control the variance of the rounding.][Fur-] thermore, the analysis of moving-average estimation is particularly challenging since the value of _αk_ is affected by all past model updates, starting from the very first iteration.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

## 5.1 SETUP

We empirically compare our IntSGD algorithm with several representative and strong baselines: SGD, Heuristic IntSGD (Sapio et al., 2021), SGD, PowerSGD + Error-feedback (EF) (Vogels et al., 2019), NatSGD (Horváth et al., 2019), and QSGD (Alistarh et al., 2017). The experiments are performed on 16 NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPUs located on 8 compute nodes of a cluster (2 GPUs per node) following the PowerSGD paper. The compute nodes in the cluster utilize InfiniBand HDR-100 Director Switch at 100Gbps speed for network connection. The cluster also supports the NVIDIA Collective Communications Library (NCCL).

7

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

We consider two tasks: image classification by ResNet18 (He et al., 2016) on the CIFAR-10 dataset and language modeling by a 3-layer LSTM on the Wikitext-2 dataset. The neural network architectures and hyperparameters are from some public PyTorch implementations[3] . Our code is built on the codebase of PowerSGD[4] . We also borrow their all-reduce-based implementations of SGD and PowerSGD. It is worth noting that QSGD and NatSGD do not support all-reduce. Thus, we implement their collective communications by all-gather. The implementations for compression and decompression in QSGD and NatSGD are from the authors of NatSGD[5] . For the sake of comparison, we also implement the all-gather-based SGD. We report the results of 3 repetitions with varying seeds.

Apart from the IntSGD with randomized integer rounding (IntSGD (Random)) analyzed in our theory, we also consider the variant of IntSGD with deterministic integer rounding (IntSGD (Determ.)) which can use the PyTorch built-in function torch.round. For all IntSGD variants, we clip the local stochastic gradients to ensure that each aggregated value fits in either 8 bits or 32 bits.

For more details of the experimental setup, please refer to Appendix C.1.

## 5.3 INTSGD VS. OTHER BASELINES

We also compare our IntSGD algorithms to the other baselines including the all-gather-based SGD, QSGD, NatSGD and the all-reduce-based SGD, PowerSGD (EF) on the two tasks. See the test performance and time breakdown in Table 2 and Table 3.

First, we can observe that the all-gather based SGD + compressors (e.g., QSGD, NatSGD) are indeed faster than the all-gather based full-precision SGD, which shows the benefit of lossy compressions. However, they are even much slower than the all-reduce-based full-precision SGD. Unfortunately, QSGD and NatSGD) does not support the more efficient all-reduce primitive. Similar observation can be seen in previous works (Vogels et al., 2019; Agarwal et al., 2021).

All of PowerSGD (EF), IntSGD (Random), and IntSGD (Determ.) are consistently faster than the allreduce-based full-precision SGD on both tasks. Compared to IntSGD (Determ.), IntSGD (Random) leads to slightly more computation overhead due to the randomized rounding. However, IntSGD

## 6 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we propose the provably convergent and computationally cheap IntSGD algorithm for efficient distributed machine learning. The core component of IntSGD is the adaptively estimated scaling factor shared by all users, which makes it compatible with the widely used communication primitive all-reduce and the recently proposed in-network aggregation (INA) (Sapio et al., 2021). The convergence rates of IntSGD match that of SGD up to constant factors on a broad spectrum of problems. Experimental results on two deep learning tasks show its promising empirical performance. A limitation of our algorithm is that its compression ratio is bounded by 4, but we hope to address this in a future work.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and well-motivated contribution to distributed optimization by addressing the critical gap between theoretical convergence guarantees and the practical constraints of hardware-accelerated communication (specifically In-Network Aggregation). The writing is exceptionally clear, demonstrating a command of the trade-offs between communication efficiency, computational overhead, and convergence stability. The most significant strength is the authors' ability to identify a concrete weakness in existing state-of-the-art heuristics (SwitchML) and replace them with a mathematically rigorous, adaptive mechanism that maintains the utility of integer-based communication. The paper is well-structured, moving logically from the limitations of current compression techniques—such as their incompatibility with efficient aggregation primitives and the burdensome memory requirements of error-feedback—to the proposed algorithmic solution. Potential reviewers should look closely at whether the "adaptive" scaling mechanisms maintain their performance stability in high-noise regimes, as this is the primary area where the theory will encounter its most significant real-world challenges. Overall, the exposition is professional, and the motivation for the contribution is both timely and practically significant.

comments.quote: Note that E [ Int ( t )] = ( t − [ t ])([ t ] + 1) + ([ t ] + 1 − t )[ t ] = t.

The equation becomes (t - k)(k + 1) + (k + 1 - t)(k). This expands to: tk + t - k^2 - k + k^2 + k - tk = t. The authors swapped the probability weights (the term multiplying [t] is 1-f, whereas the text shows it multiplying [t] but assigns the 'f' factor to [t]+1). Algebraically, $(t - \lfloor t \rfloor)(\lfloor t \rfloor + 1) + (1 - t + \lfloor t \rfloor)\lfloor t \rfloor$ simplifies to $t(\lfloor t \rfloor + 1) - \lfloor t \rfloor^2 - t\lfloor t \rfloor + \lfloor t \rfloor + \lfloor t \rfloor - t\lfloor t \rfloor + \lfloor t \rfloor^2 = ...$ wait, let me re-calculate: $(t - \lfloor t \rfloor)(\lfloor t \rfloor + 1) + (1 - (t - \lfloor t \rfloor))\lfloor t \rfloor = t\lfloor t \rfloor + t - \lfloor t \rfloor^2 - \lfloor t \rfloor + \lfloor t \rfloor - t\lfloor t \rfloor + \lfloor t \rfloor^2 = t$. This expands to $\lfloor t \rfloor^2 + \lfloor t \rfloor - t\lfloor t \rfloor$. The full sum is $(t - \lfloor t \rfloor)(\lfloor t \rfloor + 1) + (\lfloor t \rfloor + 1 - t)\lfloor t \rfloor$. This is actually summing the probability of rounding UP times the value UP, plus the probability of rounding DOWN times the value DOWN. The authors wrote $(\lfloor t \rfloor + 1 - t)\lfloor t \rfloor$ where they likely meant $(1 - (t - \lfloor t \rfloor))\lfloor t \rfloor$. progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

The paper presents a well-motivated and technically rigorous contribution to the field of distributed optimization, addressing a genuine gap between theoretical compression frameworks and real-world hardware constraints—particularly the integer-only arithmetic capabilities of programmable switches. The exposition is generally clear, with a strong narrative that contrasts the proposed *Adaptive IntSGD* against the heuristic-based SwitchML approach and situates it within the broader compression literature. However, several significant issues warrant attention. First, the theoretical contributions are presented in a highly compressed manner; the manuscript would benefit from a more detailed exposition of the adaptive scaling rule derivation and its convergence proofs, as the current treatment may leave readers questioning the tightness of the constants and the practical implications of the theoretical guarantees. Second, while the paper claims compatibility with both in-network aggregation and all-reduce, the experimental evaluation appears disproportionately focused on synthetic or smaller-scale tasks, raising concerns about the method's scalability and empirical performance on the large-scale deep learning benchmarks that motivate the work. Third, the narrative would be strengthened by a more thorough discussion of the practical overheads—specifically, the computational cost of the adaptive scaling mechanism relative to the heuristic baseline, and how the method behaves under bandwidth-constrained or heterogeneous network conditions. Addressing these issues would substantially improve the paper's impact and persuasiveness.

comments.title: Multiple issues with the adaptive scaling factor definition (OCR errors, inconsistency, ambiguity, vector/scalar mismatch, and underspecified parameters)
comments.quote: α_k = ~~_√_~~ 2 _nr_k/η_k_[2] + _[ε]_[2]  (from Proposition 2) vs α_k = ~~_√_~~ _d_ ~~_√_~~ 2 _nr_k/η_k_[2] + _[ε]_[2] (from Algorithm 1); and the text defines α_k as a vector in R^d but computes a scalar.
comments.explanation: The paper contains several issues with the definition of the adaptive scaling factor α_k. First, the formula suffers from OCR errors (e.g., tilde strikes for square roots, stray brackets). Second, the formula in Proposition 2 omits the dimension d that appears in Algorithm 1, creating an inconsistency. Third, the parentheses grouping in Algorithm 1 is ambiguous (e.g., α_k = √d √(2 n r_k/(η_k^2 + ε^2)) or √d √(2 n r_k)/(η_k^2 + ε^2)?). Fourth, α_k is introduced as a vector in R^d but the formula computes a scalar, leaving it unclear whether it is broadcast or should vary per coordinate. Fifth, the dependence on β and ε (used in computing r_k and the safeguard) is not theoretically justified; it is unclear if default values (β=0.9, ε=1e-8) satisfy Assumption 1. These issues together make it difficult to reproduce the algorithm and verify the theory.

Formally, to train a model, we want to solve the problem of parallel/distributed minimization of the average of _n_ functions:

where we will compute the gradients of stochastic realizations _fi_ ( _x_ ; _ξ_ ).

The text reads 'average of _n_ functions:' followed by a blank line, then 'where we will compute...'. This appears to be either an OCR error that dropped the formula or an incomplete insertion. Since the formula is fundamental to defining the problem, its absence is confusing. However, the rest of the paper likely contains the full definition elsewhere, so this may be a formatting artifact. Nevertheless, in this extracted context, the reader cannot see the precise optimization problem.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about IntSGD supporting all-reduce
comments.quote: Thus, IntSGD supports the efficient allreduce primitive while QSGD does not.
comments.explanation: The text correctly contrasts IntSGD with QSGD on the basis that IntSGD uses a shared scaling factor α_k across workers, enabling all-reduce, while QSGD uses per-worker normalization factors. However, the claim 'IntSGD supports the efficient allreduce primitive' could be misleading: the integer rounding operator produces integer vectors that can be summed via all-reduce without decompression, but the method still requires that the communication infrastructure (e.g., switch or all-reduce implementation) can handle integer accumulation. The text acknowledges this context earlier (SwitchML), but the phrasing here may lead a reader to think all-reduce is automatically efficient without any hardware support. This is a minor framing concern.

The actual bounds for Δ_k and f(x̂_k) - f(x^*) are not fully displayed in the extracted text (they are cut off). The reader cannot verify the stated rates or see the exact dependence on parameters. While this is likely due to the extraction (the full paper would have the complete formulas), the current passage leaves key results as incomplete statements. comments.title: Questionable claim about EF-SGD convergence compared to IntSGD
comments.quote: Compared to their result, our rate is never worse and does not require the second moment of stochastic gradient to be bounded, which is often violated in practice even for quadratic objectives and simplest neural networks.
comments.explanation: The authors claim IntSGD's rate (from Corollary 2) is 'never worse' than EF-SGD's O(σ/√(kn) + (G/k)^{2/3}) rate from Koloskova et al. (2019). However, the comparison is incomplete: the EF-SGD analysis typically requires an additional assumption on the biased compressor's contraction (e.g., δ < 1), whereas IntSGD's analysis relies on Assumption 1 (the variance bound on the rounding operator) which is controlled by the adaptive scaling. The claim 'never worse' depends on the specific constant factors and problem parameters, which are not explicitly compared. Moreover, the statement that bounded second moment 'is often violated in practice' is asserted without citation or evidence. This overstates what has been shown, as the comparison is between different assumptions and settings. The authors should qualify the comparison.

comments.title: Misleading claim about variance of integer compressor
comments.quote: the variance of the integer compressor will not decrease to zero when ∥g∥^2→0
comments.explanation: The authors contrast IntSGD with QSGD, stating that QSGD's variance satisfies E[∥Q(g)-g∥²] ≤ ω∥g∥², so it goes to zero as ∥g∥→0, while Int does not satisfy this and 'will not decrease to zero when ∥g∥²→0'. However, for fixed α, as ∥g∥→0, the stochastic rounding output Int(αg) tends to 0 with probability approaching 1, so the variance of Int(αg) also tends to 0. Even after rescaling by 1/α, the variance of Q(g) = (1/α)Int(αg) behaves like O(∥g∥/α), which still goes to zero. The intended point is that the variance does not vanish proportionally to ∥g∥² (it vanishes linearly), not that it fails to vanish at all. The wording is imprecise and could mislead a careful reader into thinking variance remains bounded away from zero.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in compression efficiency example
comments.quote: For instance, for d ∼ 10^{10} and n ∼ 100, the upper bound is 1 + log2( √5 · 10^7 ) < 14 bits.
comments.explanation: The example claims that with d ≈ 10¹⁰ and n ≈ 100, the bit width is less than 14 bits. However, evaluating the bound from the derivation: if α_k ≈ √(2n)∥g_i∥/√d then ∥α_k g_i∥_∞ ≤ √(2n) ≈ 14.14, corresponding to about 4.8 bits. If instead the bound used is √(2dn), then √(2·10¹⁰·100) ≈ 1.414×10⁶, yielding about 21.4 bits. The stated value √5·10⁷ ≈ 2.236×10⁷, with log2 ≈ 24.4, plus 1 gives 25.4 bits, not 14 bits. The number 14 bits would match d ~ 10⁶ rather than 10¹⁰. This suggests a likely typo in the exponent of d or a miscalculation, which could undermine the claimed bit count for large models.

comments.title: Duplicate baseline name in enumeration
comments.quote: SGD, Heuristic IntSGD (Sapio et al., 2021), SGD, PowerSGD + Error-feedback (EF) (Vogels et al., 2019), NatSGD (Horváth et al., 2019), and QSGD (Alistarh et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The list of baselines contains ‘SGD’ twice. The first occurrence likely refers to standard full-precision SGD (all-gather or all-reduce), and the second is probably a typo or mis-edit. This duplication does not affect the meaning but could confuse readers about the number of distinct baselines.

comments.title: Gradient clipping in experiments not covered by theory
comments.quote: For all IntSGD variants, we clip the local stochastic gradients to ensure that each aggregated value fits in either 8 bits or 32 bits.
comments.explanation: The theoretical analysis of IntSGD (Section 3) assumes the integer rounding operator is unbiased (Lemma 1) and that no additional modification (such as clipping) is applied to the stochastic gradients. Clipping introduces bias and may violate the variance bounds used in the convergence proofs. Although clipping is a common practical technique, the paper does not discuss how it affects the theoretical guarantees or whether the proven convergence rates still hold. This creates a gap between the claimed ‘provably convergent’ method and the actual implementation evaluated in the experiments.

comments.title: Unclear and potentially inconsistent claim about compression ratio
comments.quote: A limitation of our algorithm is that its compression ratio is bounded by 4
comments.explanation: The phrase ‘compression ratio is bounded by 4’ is ambiguous: it is not defined whether it refers to the ratio of float bits to integer bits, the ratio of original size to compressed size, or something else. Earlier in Section 4.2, the paper calculates that for typical problem dimensions (d ~ 10^10, n ~ 100) at most 14 bits per coordinate are needed, which corresponds to a compression ratio of about 2.3× relative to 32-bit floats, not 4. Additionally, the experiments use both 8-bit and 32-bit integer representations, which would give ratios of 4 and 1 respectively, so the claim ‘bounded by 4’ is either trivial (if taken as an upper bound) or inconsistent with the earlier estimate. This statement appears without justification in the conclusion and could mislead readers about the practical compression efficiency.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

The paper presents a well-motivated and technically rigorous contribution to the field of distributed optimization, addressing a genuine gap between theoretical compression frameworks and real-world hardware constraints—particularly the integer-only arithmetic capabilities of programmable switches. The exposition is generally clear, with a strong narrative that contrasts the proposed *Adaptive IntSGD* against the heuristic-based SwitchML approach and situates it within the broader compression literature. However, several significant issues warrant attention. First, the theoretical contributions are presented in a highly compressed manner; the manuscript would benefit from a more detailed exposition of the adaptive scaling rule derivation and its convergence proofs, as the current treatment may leave readers questioning the tightness of the constants and the practical implications of the theoretical guarantees. Second, while the paper claims compatibility with both in-network aggregation and all-reduce, the experimental evaluation appears disproportionately focused on synthetic or smaller-scale tasks, raising concerns about the method's scalability and empirical performance on the large-scale deep learning benchmarks that motivate the work. Third, the narrative would be strengthened by a more thorough discussion of the practical overheads—specifically, the computational cost of the adaptive scaling mechanism relative to the heuristic baseline, and how the method behaves under bandwidth-constrained or heterogeneous network conditions. Addressing these issues would substantially improve the paper's impact and persuasiveness.

comments.title: Missing formula for problem definition
comments.quote:
Formally, to train a model, we want to solve the problem of parallel/distributed minimization of the average of _n_ functions:

where we will compute the gradients of stochastic realizations _fi_ ( _x_ ; _ξ_ ).
comments.explanation: The passage states the problem but omits the actual mathematical expression for the minimization objective (the average of n functions). The text reads 'average of _n_ functions:' followed by a blank line, then 'where we will compute...'. This appears to be either an OCR error that dropped the formula or an incomplete insertion. Since the formula is fundamental to defining the problem, its absence is confusing. However, the rest of the paper likely contains the full definition elsewhere, so this may be a formatting artifact. Nevertheless, in this extracted context, the reader cannot see the precise optimization problem.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in scaling factor formula for adaptive α_k
comments.explanation: The formula for the adaptive scaling factor appears corrupted by OCR artifacts. It shows '~~_√_~~' which likely was meant to be a square root symbol, and the overall expression seems intended to be something like α_k = √(2 n r_k / (η_k^2 d^2 + ε^2)) or similar. There is also a stray '[2]' after η_k which is likely a superscript (η_k^2) misinterpreted. The same pattern appears twice. Given the clear algorithmic description in Algorithm 1 line 7: 'α_k = ~~_√_~~ _d_ ~~_√_~~ 2 _nr_k/η_k_[2] + _[ε]_[2]', the intended formula is ambiguous. However, the context and subsequent discussion (Proposition 2) indicates α_k = √(2 n r_k / (η_k^2 d^2 + ε^2)) but the notation here is inconsistent and likely OCR-mangled.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of d in scaling factor formula between text and Algorithm 1
comments.quote: α_k = ~~_√_~~ 2 _nr_k/η_k_[2] + _[ε]_[2]  (from Proposition 2 prose) vs α_k = ~~_√_~~ _d_ ~~_√_~~ 2 _nr_k/η_k_[2] + _[ε]_[2] (from Algorithm 1 line 7)
comments.explanation: In Proposition 2, the scaling factor formula omits the 'd' that appears in Algorithm 1. Algorithm 1 includes '_√_ _d_' inside the expression, while Proposition 2's text does not. This is inconsistent unless the 'd' is factored differently or absorbed. The intended formula probably is α_k = √(2 n r_k) / (η_k √(d^2 + ε^2)) or similar. A careful reader cannot tell which is correct without resolving this discrepancy. If the dimension 'd' is missing from Proposition 2, that would change the variance control in the theory. This is a real notation inconsistency even after considering OCR issues.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about IntSGD supporting all-reduce
comments.quote: Thus, IntSGD supports the efficient allreduce primitive while QSGD does not.
comments.explanation: The text correctly contrasts IntSGD with QSGD on the basis that IntSGD uses a shared scaling factor α_k across workers, enabling all-reduce, while QSGD uses per-worker normalization factors. However, the claim 'IntSGD supports the efficient allreduce primitive' could be misleading: the integer rounding operator produces integer vectors that can be summed via all-reduce without decompression, but the method still requires that the communication infrastructure (e.g., switch or all-reduce implementation) can handle integer accumulation. The text acknowledges this context earlier (SwitchML), but the phrasing here may lead a reader to think all-reduce is automatically efficient without any hardware support. This is a minor framing concern.

comments.explanation: The paper states theorems but the provided context only shows the assumptions and the final inequalities. The actual bounds for Δ_k and f(x̂_k) - f(x^*) are not fully displayed in the extracted text (they are cut off). The reader cannot verify the stated rates or see the exact dependence on parameters. While this is likely due to the extraction (the full paper would have the complete formulas), the current passage leaves key results as incomplete statements. This is a formatting issue but could confuse a reviewer trying to evaluate the claims without the full paper.

This underspecification makes it hard to reproduce the method's behavior.

It is ambiguous whether the intended expression is αk = √d · √(2 n r_k) / (η_k^2 + ε^2) or αk = √d · √(2 n r_k / (η_k^2 + ε^2)), or possibly something else. progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling contribution by introducing IntSGD, a provably convergent, adaptive integer compression scheme for distributed SGD that resolves the convergence ambiguities and profiling dependencies of prior SwitchML-based methods (Sapio et al., 2021). The theoretical quality is high, offering iteration complexity guarantees matching SGD across diverse settings (convex/non-convex, smooth/nonsmooth, overparameterization), while the algorithmic design is noted for its computational efficiency and compatibility with primitives like all-reduce. The writing is generally clear and effectively motivates the work by highlighting limitations in existing compressors, such as incompatibility with efficient all-reduce and memory burdens of error feedback. However, the most significant issue lies in the clarity of the "compression" claim: the manuscript emphasizes scaling integers to fit within 32 bits for hardware switches, which obscures whether the method yields genuine bandwidth reduction compared to float transmission or primarily optimizes compute latency and in-network aggregation; the authors must explicitly clarify the resulting bit-width and distinguish between bandwidth savings and compute/latency benefits. Furthermore, the practical impact of the "adaptive" scaling should be scrutinized regarding overhead; the paper should rigorously demonstrate that the cost of estimating scale factors does not introduce prohibitive synchronization or communication penalties, particularly to validate the claim of being "computationally cheaper" than the heuristic baseline.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in Algorithm 1 update step
comments.quote: Update the model parameter _x[k]_[+1] = _x[k] − ηkg[k]_
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, line 12 explicitly defines and computes the gradient estimator as `_g_ ˜ _[k]_` (i.e., $\tilde{g}^{[k]}$), which incorporates the compressed and aggregated integer vectors. However, line 13 uses `g[k]` (i.e., $g^{[k]}$) in the model update step. This is a notation inconsistency. Since $g^{[k]}$ typically denotes the raw local stochastic gradient (as defined in Section 2.2), using it here would contradict the algorithm's design of communicating only scaled/rounded integers. The update should consistently use the estimator $\tilde{g}^{[k]}$. This could mislead readers or implementers into using raw gradients instead of the compressed updates.

comments.title: Quantitative mismatch in bit complexity calculation
comments.quote: For instance, for _d ∼_ 10[10] and _n ∼_ 100, the upper bound is 1 + log2( _√_ 5 _·_ 10[7] ) _<_ 14 bits.
comments.explanation: The authors derive a bound for the maximum per-coordinate magnitude of the scaled gradient: $\|\alpha_k g_i^{[k]}\|_\infty \leq \sqrt{\frac{2n}{d}} \|g_i^{[k]}\|_2$. Under standard assumptions where gradient norms scale with dimension (e.g., $\|g\|_2 \sim \sqrt{d}$ for i.i.d. components), this bound simplifies to $\approx \sqrt{2n} \approx 14$ for $n=100$, requiring roughly 4-5 bits. To obtain a magnitude of $\sqrt{5 \cdot 10^7} \approx 7071$ (which leads to the stated 14 bits), one would need $\|g\|_2 \sim d$, or the dimension $d$ to be around $10^5$ rather than $10^{10}$. The explicit claim of $d \sim 10^{10}$ combined with the 14-bit figure suggests a typo in either the dimensionality assumption, the gradient norm bound, or the bit calculation itself. This ambiguity directly impacts the practical compression efficiency claims for large-scale models.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing in SwitchML description obscures mathematical operation
comments.quote: To increase the rounding precision, the gradient _gi[k]_[on device] _[ i]_[ multiplies a positive] scaling factor _αk_ known to every worker and then rounded to an integer number _Int_ ( _αk ◦ gi[k]_[)][.][As]
comments.explanation: The sentence structure is grammatically broken and mathematically unclear due to a mix of OCR artifacts and awkward phrasing. It reads as if the gradient 'multiplies a scaling factor' (instead of 'multiplies by') and 'then rounded' (instead of 'and then rounds it'). While the intended operation $\text{Int}(\alpha_k \circ g_i^{[k]})$ is visible, the prose fails to cleanly separate the scalar/vector multiplication step from the rounding step. A careful reader might initially question whether $\alpha_k$ is a scalar or vector, or whether the rounding applies to the product or just the gradient. Clarifying the syntax would improve reproducibility and readability without altering the technical content.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 notation inconsistency between lines 12 and 13
comments.quote:
12: Compute the (sub)gradient estimator: _g_ ˜ _[k]_ = _nα_ 1 _k_  _ni_ =1 _[Q]_[(] _[g] i[k]_[)] ˜
13: Update the model parameter _x[k]_[+1] = _x[k] − ηkg[k]_
comments.explanation: Line 12 explicitly defines the (sub)gradient estimator as $\tilde{g}[k]$, but Line 13 uses $g[k]$ in the parameter update step. Given the surrounding context and standard distributed SGD notation, this is almost certainly a typo for $\tilde{g}[k]$. While the intent is clear, the mismatch between the defined estimator and its usage in the update rule creates a superficial inconsistency that could confuse a careful reader or implementation reviewer.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in bit-complexity example (Section 4.2)
comments.quote: For instance, for d ∼ 10[10] and n ∼ 100, the upper bound is 1 + log2( √ 5 · 10[7] ) < 14 bits.
comments.explanation: The preceding analysis in Section 4.2 implies that the maximum scaled integer value scales with $\sqrt{2dn}$ (after resolving the OCR artifacts in the formula). For the stated parameters $d \sim 10^{10}$ and $n \sim 100$, we have $2dn = 2 \cdot 10^{12}$, and $\sqrt{2dn} \approx 1.414 \cdot 10^6$. The base-2 logarithm of this value is $\approx 20.4$, which corresponds to $\sim 21$ bits (plus sign), not $<14$ bits. The claimed value $\sqrt{5} \cdot 10^7 \approx 7.07 \cdot 10^6$ would require $dn \approx 2.5 \cdot 10^{14}$, contradicting the explicitly stated dimensions. This numerical mismatch undermines the concrete efficiency claim for the provided example, likely due to a typo in the parameters or the bound formula.

comments.title: Minor grammatical typo in Section 2.2
comments.quote: We left the design of _αk_ to Section 4.
comments.explanation: The verb 'left' should be 'leave' to match the present tense used throughout the paper ('We observe', 'We propose', 'We develop'). This is a superficial editorial oversight that does not affect technical content but slightly detracts from polish.

comments.title: Inconsistency in scaling factor dependence on step size between Proposition 2 and Section 4.2
comments.explanation: In Proposition 2, the adaptive scaling factor is defined as $\alpha_k = \sqrt{\frac{d}{2}} \sqrt{\frac{n r_k}{\eta_k} + \varepsilon^2}$, which scales with $1/\sqrt{\eta_k}$. However, in Section 4.2, the derivation for bit complexity states: "The adaptive scheme with $\beta = 0$, $\varepsilon = 0$ gives $\alpha_k = \sqrt{\frac{d}{2n}} \|\eta_k x^{[k]} - x^{[k-1]}\| \dots \approx \sqrt{\frac{d}{2n}} \|\eta_k x^{[k+1]} - x^{[k]}\| = \sqrt{\frac{d}{2n}} \|g_i^{[k]}\|$." This implies $\alpha_k$ scales linearly with $\eta_k$ (or becomes independent of it after substitution), which directly contradicts the $1/\sqrt{\eta_k}$ dependence in Proposition 2. This discrepancy undermines the subsequent bit-complexity claim, as the scaling of $\alpha_k$ critically determines both the variance of the gradient estimator and the number of representable integers. Even accounting for OCR artifacts, the structural mismatch in $\eta_k$ dependence is mathematically significant.

comments.title: Bit complexity bound lacks gradient norm constant, leading to nonsensical negative bit counts
comments.quote: 1 + log_2 ~~_√√_~~ 2 _dn_[bits for each coordinate.
comments.explanation: The text claims that the number of bits per coordinate is bounded by $1 + \log_2(\sqrt{\dots})$. Based on the surrounding derivation, the intended bound is likely $1 + \log_2(\sqrt{\frac{d}{2n}} G)$, where $G$ bounds $\|g_i^{[k]}\|_\infty$. However, the $G$ (or any scale factor) is omitted. For large dimensions (e.g., $d \sim 10^{10}, n \sim 100$), $\sqrt{d/(2n)} \approx 7071$, yielding a positive log, but the expression as written (or interpreted as $\sqrt{2/(dn)}$) is strictly less than 1 for practically relevant $d,n$, resulting in a negative logarithm and thus a negative number of bits, which is physically impossible. The omission of the gradient norm bound or an assumption $G=1$ without explicit justification creates ambiguity that could mislead reproducibility.

comments.title: Garbled and potentially inaccurate comparison rate for EF-SGD
comments.quote: O( ~~_√_~~ _σkn_[+] ~~_√_~~ _Gk_ ~~_√_~~ _2 _/_ 3[] _rate in Koloskova et al. (2019) when assuming the second moment of stochastic gradient is bounded by G^2.
comments.explanation: The convergence rate claimed for EF-SGD (Koloskova et al., 2019) in the non-convex smooth case is heavily garbled by OCR and structurally inconsistent with known results. Standard results for biased compressed SGD in this regime yield $\mathcal{O}(k^{-1/3})$ or $\mathcal{O}((\sigma_*/kn)^{1/2} + k^{-1/3})$. The displayed expression appears to mix dimensions (e.g., $\sqrt{\sigma_* k/n}$ grows with $k$, contradicting convergence). Even if this is an OCR artifact, the intended comparison term seems to misstate the baseline complexity. If corrected to a growing rate, it would falsely imply IntSGD's convergence is unimprovable; if it was meant to be decaying, the missing inverse $k$ dependencies must be fixed to accurately contextualize the paper's theoretical advantage.

comments.title: Contradictory definitions of the adaptive scaling factor α_k
comments.quote:
7: Compute the adaptive scaling factor: _αk_ = ~~_√_~~ _d_ ~~_√_~~ 2 _nrk/ηk_[2][+] _[ε]_[2]

**Proposition 2.** Assumption 1 holds if we choose _β ∈_ [0 _,_ 1) _, ε ≥_ 0 and _αk_ = ~~_√_~~ 2 _nr_ ~~_√_~~ _k/ηd k_[2][+] _[ε]_[2] _[,]_[ where _rk_ = _βrk−_ 1 + (1 _− β_ ) _∥x[k] − x[k][−]_[1] _∥_[2] _._
comments.explanation: Initially, the OCR artifacts (~~_√_~~, spacing) make the formulas look messy, but stripping the formatting reveals a critical mathematical contradiction between the algorithm pseudocode and the theoretical proposition. Algorithm 1 (line 7) places the dimension $d$ in the numerator outside the square root (likely $\alpha_k = \sqrt{d} \sqrt{\frac{2 n r_k}{\eta_k} + \varepsilon^2}$), whereas Proposition 2 places $d$ in the denominator inside the square root (likely $\alpha_k = \sqrt{\frac{2 n r_k}{\eta_k d} + \varepsilon^2}$). These definitions differ by a factor of $d$ in the scaling factor. In compressed gradient methods, the scaling factor is typically chosen to counteract variance growth with dimension, making the $1/\sqrt{d}$ scaling in Proposition 2 theoretically consistent. The $\sqrt{d}$ scaling in Algorithm 1 would cause the integer representation range to grow with dimension, potentially destabilizing training and violating the intended variance control. This discrepancy directly impacts reproducibility and suggests a typographical or conceptual error in one of the statements.

comments.title: Slight overstatement of performance difference on LSTM task
comments.quote: Compared to PowerSGD (EF), our IntSGD variants are better on the task of training ResNet18 but inferior on the task of training a 3-layer LSTM.
comments.explanation: Reading the claim alongside Table 3, PowerSGD (EF) reports a test loss of 4.52 ± 0.01, while IntSGD (Random) reports 4.54 ± 0.01. The absolute difference is 0.02, which falls entirely within the reported standard deviations. While numerically higher, characterizing this as 'inferior' may overstate the practical distinction, as the results are statistically indistinguishable. The wording could mislead readers into thinking there is a meaningful degradation in language modeling performance when the evidence only supports a marginal, non-significant difference. Qualifying this as 'marginally worse' or 'comparable' would better reflect the data.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling contribution by introducing IntSGD, a provably convergent, adaptive integer compression scheme for distributed SGD that resolves the convergence ambiguities and profiling dependencies of prior SwitchML-based methods (Sapio et al., 2021). The theoretical quality is high, offering iteration complexity guarantees matching SGD across diverse settings (convex/non-convex, smooth/nonsmooth, overparameterization), while the algorithmic design is noted for its computational efficiency and compatibility with primitives like all-reduce. The writing is generally clear and effectively motivates the work by highlighting limitations in existing compressors, such as incompatibility with efficient all-reduce and memory burdens of error feedback. However, the most significant issue lies in the clarity of the "compression" claim: the manuscript emphasizes scaling integers to fit within 32 bits for hardware switches, which obscures whether the method yields genuine bandwidth reduction compared to float transmission or primarily optimizes compute latency and in-network aggregation; the authors must explicitly clarify the resulting bit-width and distinguish between bandwidth savings and compute/latency benefits. Furthermore, the practical impact of the "adaptive" scaling should be scrutinized regarding overhead; the paper should rigorously demonstrate that the cost of estimating scale factors does not introduce prohibitive synchronization or communication penalties, particularly to validate the claim of being "computationally cheaper" than the heuristic baseline.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in Algorithm 1 update step
comments.quote: Update the model parameter _x[k]_[+1] = _x[k] − ηkg[k]_
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, line 12 explicitly defines and computes the gradient estimator as `_g_ ˜ _[k]_` (i.e., $\tilde{g}^{[k]}$), which incorporates the compressed and aggregated integer vectors. However, line 13 uses `g[k]` (i.e., $g^{[k]}$) in the model update step. This is a notation inconsistency. Since $g^{[k]}$ typically denotes the raw local stochastic gradient (as defined in Section 2.2), using it here would contradict the algorithm's design of communicating only scaled/rounded integers. The update should consistently use the estimator $\tilde{g}^{[k]}$. This could mislead readers or implementers into using raw gradients instead of the compressed updates.

comments.title: Quantitative mismatch in bit complexity calculation
comments.quote: For instance, for _d ∼_ 10[10] and _n ∼_ 100, the upper bound is 1 + log2( _√_ 5 _·_ 10[7] ) _<_ 14 bits.
comments.explanation: The authors derive a bound for the maximum per-coordinate magnitude of the scaled gradient: $\|\alpha_k g_i^{[k]}\|_\infty \leq \sqrt{\frac{2n}{d}} \|g_i^{[k]}\|_2$. Under standard assumptions where gradient norms scale with dimension (e.g., $\|g\|_2 \sim \sqrt{d}$ for i.i.d. components), this bound simplifies to $\approx \sqrt{2n} \approx 14$ for $n=100$, requiring roughly 4-5 bits. To obtain a magnitude of $\sqrt{5 \cdot 10^7} \approx 7071$ (which leads to the stated 14 bits), one would need $\|g\|_2 \sim d$, or the dimension $d$ to be around $10^5$ rather than $10^{10}$. The explicit claim of $d \sim 10^{10}$ combined with the 14-bit figure suggests a typo in either the dimensionality assumption, the gradient norm bound, or the bit calculation itself. This ambiguity directly impacts the practical compression efficiency claims for large-scale models.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing in SwitchML description obscures mathematical operation
comments.quote: To increase the rounding precision, the gradient _gi[k]_[on device] _[ i]_[ multiplies a positive] scaling factor _αk_ known to every worker and then rounded to an integer number _Int_ ( _αk ◦ gi[k]_[)][.][As]
comments.explanation: The sentence structure is grammatically broken and mathematically unclear due to a mix of OCR artifacts and awkward phrasing. It reads as if the gradient 'multiplies a scaling factor' (instead of 'multiplies by') and 'then rounded' (instead of 'and then rounds it'). While the intended operation $\text{Int}(\alpha_k \circ g_i^{[k]})$ is visible, the prose fails to cleanly separate the scalar/vector multiplication step from the rounding step. A careful reader might initially question whether $\alpha_k$ is a scalar or vector, or whether the rounding applies to the product or just the gradient. Clarifying the syntax would improve reproducibility and readability without altering the technical content.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 notation inconsistency between lines 12 and 13
comments.quote:
12: Compute the (sub)gradient estimator: _g_ ˜ _[k]_ = _nα_ 1 _k_  _ni_ =1 _[Q]_[(] _[g] i[k]_[)] ˜
13: Update the model parameter _x[k]_[+1] = _x[k] − ηkg[k]_
comments.explanation: Line 12 explicitly defines the (sub)gradient estimator as $\tilde{g}[k]$, but Line 13 uses $g[k]$ in the parameter update step. Given the surrounding context and standard distributed SGD notation, this is almost certainly a typo for $\tilde{g}[k]$. While the intent is clear, the mismatch between the defined estimator and its usage in the update rule creates a superficial inconsistency that could confuse a careful reader or implementation reviewer.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in bit-complexity example (Section 4.2)
comments.quote: For instance, for d ∼ 10[10] and n ∼ 100, the upper bound is 1 + log2( √ 5 · 10[7] ) < 14 bits.
comments.explanation: The preceding analysis in Section 4.2 implies that the maximum scaled integer value scales with $\sqrt{2dn}$ (after resolving the OCR artifacts in the formula). For the stated parameters $d \sim 10^{10}$ and $n \sim 100$, we have $2dn = 2 \cdot 10^{12}$, and $\sqrt{2dn} \approx 1.414 \cdot 10^6$. The base-2 logarithm of this value is $\approx 20.4$, which corresponds to $\sim 21$ bits (plus sign), not $<14$ bits. The claimed value $\sqrt{5} \cdot 10^7 \approx 7.07 \cdot 10^6$ would require $dn \approx 2.5 \cdot 10^{14}$, contradicting the explicitly stated dimensions. This numerical mismatch undermines the concrete efficiency claim for the provided example, likely due to a typo in the parameters or the bound formula.

comments.title: Minor grammatical typo in Section 2.2
comments.quote: We left the design of _αk_ to Section 4.
comments.explanation: The verb 'left' should be 'leave' to match the present tense used throughout the paper ('We observe', 'We propose', 'We develop'). This is a superficial editorial oversight that does not affect technical content but slightly detracts from polish.

comments.title: Inconsistency in scaling factor dependence on step size between Proposition 2 and Section 4.2
comments.explanation: In Proposition 2, the adaptive scaling factor is defined as $\alpha_k = \sqrt{\frac{d}{2}} \sqrt{\frac{n r_k}{\eta_k} + \varepsilon^2}$, which scales with $1/\sqrt{\eta_k}$. However, in Section 4.2, the derivation for bit complexity states: "The adaptive scheme with $\beta = 0$, $\varepsilon = 0$ gives $\alpha_k = \sqrt{\frac{d}{2n}} \|\eta_k x^{[k]} - x^{[k-1]}\| \dots \approx \sqrt{\frac{d}{2n}} \|\eta_k x^{[k+1]} - x^{[k]}\| = \sqrt{\frac{d}{2n}} \|g_i^{[k]}\|$." This implies $\alpha_k$ scales linearly with $\eta_k$ (or becomes independent of it after substitution), which directly contradicts the $1/\sqrt{\eta_k}$ dependence in Proposition 2. This discrepancy undermines the subsequent bit-complexity claim, as the scaling of $\alpha_k$ critically determines both the variance of the gradient estimator and the number of representable integers. Even accounting for OCR artifacts, the structural mismatch in $\eta_k$ dependence is mathematically significant.

comments.title: Bit complexity bound lacks gradient norm constant, leading to nonsensical negative bit counts
comments.quote: 1 + log_2 ~~_√√_~~ 2 _dn_[bits for each coordinate.
comments.explanation: The text claims that the number of bits per coordinate is bounded by $1 + \log_2(\sqrt{\dots})$. Based on the surrounding derivation, the intended bound is likely $1 + \log_2(\sqrt{\frac{d}{2n}} G)$, where $G$ bounds $\|g_i^{[k]}\|_\infty$. However, the $G$ (or any scale factor) is omitted. For large dimensions (e.g., $d \sim 10^{10}, n \sim 100$), $\sqrt{d/(2n)} \approx 7071$, yielding a positive log, but the expression as written (or interpreted as $\sqrt{2/(dn)}$) is strictly less than 1 for practically relevant $d,n$, resulting in a negative logarithm and thus a negative number of bits, which is physically impossible. The omission of the gradient norm bound or an assumption $G=1$ without explicit justification creates ambiguity that could mislead reproducibility.

comments.title: Garbled and potentially inaccurate comparison rate for EF-SGD
comments.quote: O( ~~_√_~~ _σkn_[+] ~~_√_~~ _Gk_ ~~_√_~~ _2 _/_ 3[] _rate in Koloskova et al. (2019) when assuming the second moment of stochastic gradient is bounded by G^2.
comments.explanation: The convergence rate claimed for EF-SGD (Koloskova et al., 2019) in the non-convex smooth case is heavily garbled by OCR and structurally inconsistent with known results. Standard results for biased compressed SGD in this regime yield $\mathcal{O}(k^{-1/3})$ or $\mathcal{O}((\sigma_*/kn)^{1/2} + k^{-1/3})$. The displayed expression appears to mix dimensions (e.g., $\sqrt{\sigma_* k/n}$ grows with $k$, contradicting convergence). Even if this is an OCR artifact, the intended comparison term seems to misstate the baseline complexity. If corrected to a growing rate, it would falsely imply IntSGD's convergence is unimprovable; if it was meant to be decaying, the missing inverse $k$ dependencies must be fixed to accurately contextualize the paper's theoretical advantage.

comments.title: Contradictory definitions of the adaptive scaling factor α_k
comments.quote:
7: Compute the adaptive scaling factor: _αk_ = ~~_√_~~ _d_ ~~_√_~~ 2 _nrk/ηk_[2][+] _[ε]_[2]

**Proposition 2.** Assumption 1 holds if we choose _β ∈_ [0 _,_ 1) _, ε ≥_ 0 and _αk_ = ~~_√_~~ 2 _nr_ ~~_√_~~ _k/ηd k_[2][+] _[ε]_[2] _[,]_[ where _rk_ = _βrk−_ 1 + (1 _− β_ ) _∥x[k] − x[k][−]_[1] _∥_[2] _._
comments.explanation: Initially, the OCR artifacts (~~_√_~~, spacing) make the formulas look messy, but stripping the formatting reveals a critical mathematical contradiction between the algorithm pseudocode and the theoretical proposition. Algorithm 1 (line 7) places the dimension $d$ in the numerator outside the square root (likely $\alpha_k = \sqrt{d} \sqrt{\frac{2 n r_k}{\eta_k} + \varepsilon^2}$), whereas Proposition 2 places $d$ in the denominator inside the square root (likely $\alpha_k = \sqrt{\frac{2 n r_k}{\eta_k d} + \varepsilon^2}$). These definitions differ by a factor of $d$ in the scaling factor. In compressed gradient methods, the scaling factor is typically chosen to counteract variance growth with dimension, making the $1/\sqrt{d}$ scaling in Proposition 2 theoretically consistent. The $\sqrt{d}$ scaling in Algorithm 1 would cause the integer representation range to grow with dimension, potentially destabilizing training and violating the intended variance control. This discrepancy directly impacts reproducibility and suggests a typographical or conceptual error in one of the statements.

comments.title: Slight overstatement of performance difference on LSTM task
comments.quote: Compared to PowerSGD (EF), our IntSGD variants are better on the task of training ResNet18 but inferior on the task of training a 3-layer LSTM.
comments.explanation: Reading the claim alongside Table 3, PowerSGD (EF) reports a test loss of 4.52 ± 0.01, while IntSGD (Random) reports 4.54 ± 0.01. The absolute difference is 0.02, which falls entirely within the reported standard deviations. While numerically higher, characterizing this as 'inferior' may overstate the practical distinction, as the results are statistically indistinguishable. The wording could mislead readers into thinking there is a meaningful degradation in language modeling performance when the evidence only supports a marginal, non-significant difference. Qualifying this as 'marginally worse' or 'comparable' would better reflect the data.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This is a strong and timely submission that addresses a critical bottleneck in distributed deep learning—communication cost—and offers a methodological shift from prior work by introducing provable convergence to integer-based compression. The authors clearly articulate the practical limitations of existing techniques, such as the incompatibility of many high-ratio compressors with the efficient all-reduce primitive and the memory overhead of error feedback, and they contrast their "IntSGD" method effectively against the heuristic approach of Sapio et al. (2021). The writing style is professional and the motivation is compelling, particularly the claim that the method is "floatless" and computationally cheaper. The central significance of this paper, however, rests on the theoretical assertion that IntSGD matches the convergence rate of standard SGD up to constants across convex, non-convex, smooth, and nonsmooth function classes. The most critical feedback for this review concerns the depth of the theoretical analysis that follows; the reviewer expects a rigorous justification of how the proposed adaptive scaling mechanism eliminates the variance and bias typically associated with quantization without introducing hidden computational costs, ensuring the claims regarding both theoretical complexity and practical

comments.title: Missing dimension scaling factor in Proposition 2
comments.explanation: The text for Proposition 2 (Section 4.1) presents a formula for the adaptive scaling factor \alpha_k, but the LaTeX appears to be missing the leading \sqrt{d} factor found in the corresponding description in Algorithm 1 (Section 2) and the provided summary (e.g., \alpha_k = \sqrt{d} \sqrt{\frac{2 n r_k}{\eta_k^2} + \epsilon^2}). This factor is critical for controlling the variance and is required for the bit-per-coordinate calculation in Section 4.2. Without it, the formula is inconsistent with the rest of the paper's definitions and the derived complexity results.

comments.title: Contradiction between Heuristic Formula and Overflow Claim
comments.quote: Note that the rule of scaling factor in Heuristic IntSGD is $\alpha = n \cdot 2^{\text{nb} - 1}$... Although this scaling rule is straightforward and avoids overflows
comments.explanation: The passage describes a heuristic scaling factor $\alpha = n \cdot 2^{\text{nb}-1}$ and claims that this rule 'avoids overflows'. However, this claim is mathematically inconsistent with the formula when $\text{nb}$ represents the bitwidth of the communication format (e.g., 32 bits). For a standard bitwidth like 32, $\alpha \approx n \cdot 2^{31} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{10}$. Even with a clipped gradient of magnitude 1, the scaled integer value $Int(\alpha \cdot 1) \approx 3.4 \times 10^{10}$ exceeds the maximum value of a 32-bit unsigned integer ($\approx 4.3 \times 10^9$) and clearly overflows a 32-bit register. The factor $n$ (number of workers) increases the overflow risk further. The text suggests the rule fits in '8 bits or 32 bits', but with this scaling, it fails for all non-zero gradients in 32-bit mode. This appears to be a significant error in the description of the competitor algorithm or a potential typo in the formula itself.

comments.title: Incomplete Reference List
comments.quote:
REFERENCES
- Saurabh Agarwal...
- ... - Zeyuan Allen-Zhu ... - ... - Lisandro Dalcín ... - ... - Priya Goyal ... - ... - Robert M. Gower ... - ... - Kaiming He ... - ... - Geoffrey Hinton ... - ... - Samuel Horváth ... - ... - Gao Huang ... - ... - Zeyuan Allen-Zhu and Elad Hazan ...
comments.explanation: The provided passage contains a References section that lists only about 20 citations. However, the body of the paper (Introduction and Methods sections in the full context) cites over 30 distinct references. Missing from this bibliography are several papers explicitly mentioned in the text, including Sapio et al. (2021) regarding SwitchML, Ramesh et al. (2021) regarding compression benefits, Karimireddy et al. (2019) regarding error-feedback, Stich et al. (2018) (referenced multiple times), Johnson & Zhang (2013), Kovalev et al. (2020), Xu et al. (2020), Safaryan et al. (2020), Patil et al. (2015), Suresh et al. (2013), and Zheng et al. (2013). While the Reproducibility Statement accurately describes where the definitions and proofs are located (Sections 1, 2, 3, 5, Appendices A, B, C.1), it does not claim to list the bibliography. However, the absence of the bibliography entries from the 'REFERENCES' section constitutes a critical omission in the manuscript content provided in this passage.

comments.title: Typo in Citation Format
comments.quote: The 33th International Conference on Machine Learning
comments.explanation: The text contains a minor typographical error in the citation format for Allen-Zhu and Hazan (2016). The ordinal indicator 'th' should read 'rd' (i.e., '33rd').
